Project Title: Fortaleza Sustainable Urban Development (incl. Parque Rachel de Queiroz NBS)
Type of Action: Nature-based solutions; Urban drainage; Parks/green-blue infrastructure
Sector (Categorical): Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Urban Development & Housing; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Summary: Restoration of urban green areas and water bodies to reduce flooding and improve public spaces, including works in Rachel de Queiroz Park and basins.
Key Points: Strong E&S framework; measurable flood mitigation; replicable NBS toolkit.
Detailed Expected Impact: Reduces Fortaleza’s vulnerability to flooding and climate extremes by restoring wetlands, building floodable parks, and upgrading drainage and sewage in the Vertente Marítima basin. Improves water quality in canals and coastal zones (safe bathing index from ~20% to ~80% in target areas), creates new public green spaces and mobility options (paths, lighting, recreation), and strengthens municipal planning and land‑based revenue tools. Around 300,000 residents in vulnerable neighborhoods see better flood protection, cleaner water, and more livable public spaces.
Repayment Structure: US$73.3M IBRD sovereign‑guaranteed loan to the Municipality of Fortaleza, with a maturity of about 24 years and ~6‑year grace period. The federal government of Brazil provides the repayment guarantee. Repayment is made from municipal budget over time, complemented by local counterpart funds for additional works.
Operation & Maintenance: Municipal agencies (e.g., environment and infrastructure secretariats) operate and maintain the new drainage, parks, and wetlands. Assets like Rachel de Queiroz Park’s ponds and greenways are integrated into the city’s regular park and stormwater maintenance programs. The project also builds institutional capacity and an NBS ‘toolkit’ so local teams can replicate and maintain similar solutions elsewhere.
Co-benefits (detailed): Public health gains from reduced sewage overflows and cleaner canals/beaches; new leisure areas support physical activity and social cohesion; tree planting and wetlands improve biodiversity and urban cooling; improved aesthetics and recreational value can boost local tourism and small business activity near the park; safer, better‑lit public spaces enhance security perception.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low‑income residents of flood‑prone informal neighborhoods around Rachel de Queiroz Park and the Vertente Marítima basin, including households previously affected by frequent flooding and poor sanitation. Coastal communities and beach users benefit from cleaner bathing waters. The wider population of Fortaleza benefits from reduced flood risk on key roads and increased availability of green public spaces.
Climate Goals (detailed): Primarily an adaptation project: enhances resilience to more intense rainfall and flooding expected under climate change through nature‑based solutions and improved drainage. Mitigation co‑benefits include carbon sequestration from new vegetation and reduced methane from better wastewater handling. Supports Fortaleza’s climate action plan and Brazil’s NDC adaptation objectives by reducing disaster risk and improving water management in a major coastal city.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligns with Fortaleza’s Green Area System Master Plan and municipal climate/urban development strategies, which prioritize flood control, expanded green spaces, and social inclusion. At the national level, supports Brazil’s urban resilience and sanitation policies and the broader goal of climate‑resilient cities, while fitting within the World Bank’s Brazil Country Partnership Framework for sustainable urban development.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Land tenure and resettlement complexities around park areas (e.g., federal university land and families to be relocated) forced scope adjustments and highlighted the need for early stakeholder coordination. Macroeconomic constraints required reallocating resources. A key lesson is that combining flood control with high‑quality public spaces and NBS yields strong public support and multi‑functional value but demands robust social safeguards and clear long‑term maintenance arrangements.

Project Title: Electrification and Improvement of the São Paulo Urban Transport System (Zero‑Emission Buses)
Sector (Categorical): Transport
Summary: Scale-up of e-buses and modernized operations to cut GHGs and air pollutants; supports Climate Change Law target of fully electric fleet by 2038.
Key Points: Large-scale e-bus transition; air-quality co-benefits; local manufacturing/jobs.
Detailed Expected Impact: Transforms São Paulo’s bus system by financing large‑scale deployment of battery‑electric buses and associated infrastructure, improving service reliability and reducing emissions. Expected to significantly reduce local air pollutants and greenhouse‑gas emissions from a sector that accounts for over half of city GHGs and nearly half of local air pollution. Directly benefits over 100,000 daily transit users on targeted corridors and nearly 3 million residents living along those routes, through cleaner, quieter, more reliable buses and upgraded operations.
Repayment Structure: Parallel US$248.3M IBRD loan and US$248.3M IDB loan to the Municipality of São Paulo (sovereign‑guaranteed), with long tenors (around 15–24 years) and grace periods (~6 years). Loans are repaid from municipal budgets over time, supported by the city’s transport revenue and general revenues. The program also mobilizes private capital from bus operators and financiers (around US$125M) for e‑bus fleet and charging assets via PPP‑style agreements, repaid through service contracts.
Operation & Maintenance: SPTrans (São Paulo’s transit authority) manages planning and oversight; private bus concessionaires operate and maintain the electric buses and depot charging systems under updated contracts. The project includes capacity building for operators on electric drivetrain and battery maintenance, and investments in upgraded depots, digital fleet management, and grid connections to support long‑term O&M of the electrified network.
Co-benefits (detailed): Major public‑health gains from reduced PM and NOx emissions, especially in dense and low‑income areas; less noise along bus corridors; job creation and technology transfer in Brazil’s e‑bus manufacturing and charging industries; gender and social inclusion measures (greater women’s participation in the sector and safer, more reliable services for women users); improved service quality (punctuality, comfort) and reduced congestion through integrated corridor and operations improvements.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Daily bus users—particularly low‑income commuters and women who rely heavily on public transport—and residents living near heavily used bus corridors who will experience cleaner air and less noise. Indirect beneficiaries include the broader population of metropolitan São Paulo through improved air quality and reduced GHG emissions, and workers in the emerging electric mobility value chain.
Climate Goals (detailed): Strongly supports climate mitigation by accelerating the shift from diesel to zero‑emission buses, directly supporting São Paulo’s Climate Law mandate of a 100% zero‑emission bus fleet by 2038 and contributing to Brazil’s NDC transport mitigation efforts. Also advances a broader ‘green, resilient, inclusive’ urban agenda by modernizing public transport and encouraging mode shift away from private cars.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with São Paulo’s municipal climate and mobility plans, the National Urban Mobility Policy, Brazil’s NDC, and the World Bank/IDB country strategies, all of which emphasize sustainable urban transport and emissions reduction. The project operationalizes São Paulo’s own climate legislation on fleet decarbonization and supports national efforts to position Brazil as a leader in electric mobility.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Key risks include integrating large‑scale e‑bus technology into an existing system (grid capacity, depot reconfiguration, operational learning curve), financial sustainability given higher upfront capital costs, and complex multi‑stakeholder coordination (city, operators, utility, manufacturers, MDBs). Lessons from early pilots (over 500 e‑buses already in service) underscore the value of phased rollout, robust regulatory support, and blended finance to de‑risk adoption.

Project Title: Belo Horizonte LED Street Lighting PPP (BHIP)
Sector (Categorical): Energy
Funders: Private capital via PPP; supported by IFC/ESMAP knowledge work
Summary: Replacement of ~182,000 luminaires with LEDs; remote control on main avenues.
Key Points: ≈50% electricity cost savings; improved safety; model PPP structure.
Detailed Expected Impact: City‑wide modernization of ~182,000 streetlights from conventional lamps to LEDs, cutting electricity use for public lighting by about 50%, improving lighting quality, and enabling smart remote management. Expected impacts include significant municipal energy cost savings, brighter and more uniform lighting that enhances night‑time safety, and reduced maintenance needs due to LEDs’ longer lifespan.
Repayment Structure: Implemented via a ~R$1.4B, 20‑year PPP concession. The private SPV (BHIP) finances upfront investment and is repaid over time through availability payments funded by the municipal public lighting fee and energy savings. The city avoids large upfront borrowing; instead, it commits to performance‑linked concession payments that cover capital recovery, O&M, and investor return.
Operation & Maintenance: BHIP is contractually responsible for operating and maintaining the entire street lighting system for 20 years, including LED fixtures and remote monitoring/control systems. Performance standards (uptime, response times) are embedded in the PPP contract. After concession expiry, ownership reverts to the city along with a modern, well‑maintained lighting network.
Co-benefits (detailed): Improved public safety and perceived security at night due to better illumination; increased use of public spaces and nighttime economic activity; substantial reduction in electricity consumption and associated GHG emissions; local industrial benefits from domestic manufacture of LED fixtures; fewer maintenance truck rolls and reduced light pollution thanks to better optics.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): All residents, businesses, and visitors in Belo Horizonte who use streets and public spaces after dark. Particularly beneficial for neighborhoods that were previously under‑lit or considered unsafe at night. The municipal government benefits via lower operating costs and more predictable budgeting for lighting.
Climate Goals (detailed): Contributes to climate mitigation through large‑scale energy efficiency, reducing electricity demand for public lighting by about half and thus lowering power‑sector emissions. Aligns with broader climate and SDG goals to decarbonize cities and improve energy efficiency in municipal services.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Supports Belo Horizonte’s smart city and public safety strategies, Brazil’s National Energy Efficiency Plan, and federal efforts to promote infrastructure PPPs. Demonstrates a scalable model that other Brazilian cities are adopting for modernizing public lighting with private investment.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Risks included executing a rapid city‑wide retrofit, ensuring savings materialized as projected, and managing a complex long‑term PPP. Lessons: detailed asset inventory and planning before rollout, selecting experienced technology partners, embedding remote monitoring for efficient O&M, and structuring contracts so that energy savings and service quality incentives are aligned.

Project Title: Tietê River Depollution Program – Phase IV
Sector (Categorical): Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Instrument: IDB Invest corporate loan; IDB loan (historic phases)
Summary: Expansion of sewerage and WWTP capacity to improve water quality for 3.7M residents in RMSP.
Key Points: Large metropolitan impact; strong E&S oversight; measurable coverage KPIs.
Detailed Expected Impact: Expands sewage collection and treatment in the São Paulo Metropolitan Region by building ~200 km of new sewers, ~160 km of interceptors, and expanding key WWTPs. Expected to connect around 400,000 households to sewerage, dramatically reduce organic and pathogen loads in the Tietê River, and improve environmental and sanitary conditions for roughly 3.7 million residents in 34 municipalities. Also yields sizable GHG reductions by avoiding methane emissions from untreated wastewater.
Repayment Structure: Financed primarily through a BRL 940M (~US$200M) local‑currency corporate loan from IDB Invest to Sabesp, with half the risk backstopped by a PROPARCO guarantee, complemented by other multilateral and local funds. Sabesp repays the loan from operating cash flows (tariff revenues) over a long tenor, in line with Brazil’s New Sanitation Law universalization timeline.
Operation & Maintenance: Sabesp owns, operates, and maintains all new sewer networks and expanded WWTP capacity, integrating them into its existing metropolitan sanitation system. The project includes institutional strengthening and an Environmental and Social Action Plan to ensure high‑standard operations, sludge handling, and environmental compliance over time.
Co-benefits (detailed): Major public‑health improvements from reduced exposure to raw sewage and contaminated floodwaters; revived river ecosystems and biodiversity; contribution to urban flood risk reduction; enhanced quality of life and property values in upgraded neighborhoods; climate co‑benefits from reduced methane and a cleaner, more resilient water environment.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Roughly 400,000 newly connected households—predominantly low‑income residents in peri‑urban municipalities—as well as 3.7 million people who benefit from improved river and environmental conditions across the metro area. Downstream users and ecosystems also benefit from cleaner river water.
Climate Goals (detailed): Classified as almost fully aligned with climate finance, delivering both mitigation (large methane reductions) and adaptation (more resilient sanitation and flood‑resistant urban environment). Supports Brazil’s NDC and Paris‑alignment by decarbonizing the wastewater sector and building water resilience in a mega‑city.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Directly supports Brazil’s New Sanitation Law goal of universal sewage by 2033 and the long‑standing Tietê River cleanup agenda of São Paulo. Aligns with national water and environmental policies, SDG 6, and IDB/IDB Invest strategies to promote sustainable, climate‑aligned urban infrastructure.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Challenges include executing large works in dense urban areas (land acquisition, traffic disruption), ensuring robust environmental and social safeguards, and managing long‑term financial sustainability. Early lessons: flexible project scope (e.g., adding Franco da Rocha WWTP), strong ESAP implementation, and local‑currency financing are critical for success in large utility‑led climate infrastructure.

Project Title: PROSAMIM / PROSAMIN – Social & Environmental Program for Igarapés
Type of Action: Flood risk reduction; Sanitation; Slum upgrading
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Urban Development & Housing; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Intermediaries/Implementers: Government of Amazonas; Municipality of Manaus
Status: Multiple phases since 2006; Phase III approved 2012; IV in preparation
Summary: Resettlement from flood-prone areas; drainage canals; sewer connections; public spaces to reduce flood and health risks.
Key Points: Integrated social + resilience approach; extensive KPIs; long-running program.
Detailed Expected Impact: Multi‑phase slum upgrading and creek restoration program in Manaus that relocates thousands of families from flood‑prone igarapé settlements into safe housing, constructs drainage and sanitation infrastructure, and reclaims degraded creek corridors as roads and parks. Expected impacts include sharply reduced flood risk, improved health and sanitation, safer housing, and environmental revitalization of urban waterways.
Repayment Structure: Financed through successive sovereign loans from the IDB (e.g., PROSAMIM III ~US$280M IDB loan + US$120M local counterpart), with 25‑year maturity, 5‑year grace, and LIBOR‑based interest. The State of Amazonas repays these loans, guaranteed by the federal government, from its budget over the long term, supported by national programs such as the Growth Acceleration Program.
Operation & Maintenance: Post‑construction, state and municipal agencies share O&M: drainage canals and flood works are maintained by state infrastructure or city works departments; sewer and water networks are operated by the city’s water utility; new parks and public spaces are managed by the municipality; housing is transferred to residents and local housing authorities. The PROSAMIM project unit (UGPE) helped set up arrangements and train institutions for long‑term maintenance.
Co-benefits (detailed): Health gains from reduced flooding and better sanitation; large improvements in housing quality and security of tenure; creation of linear parks and public spaces that enhance recreation and urban cooling; increased property values and economic opportunities in regenerated areas; strengthened institutional capacity for integrated urban planning; advances in social inclusion by bringing marginalized creekside communities into the formal city.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low‑income families formerly living in precarious palafitas along igarapés, plus additional residents in nearby neighborhoods who benefit from new infrastructure and public spaces. Tens of thousands of people see dramatic improvements in safety, housing, sanitation, and urban amenities.
Climate Goals (detailed): Predominantly an adaptation/resilience project: reduces exposure to climate‑amplified flooding and improves drainage in a rainforest city. Mitigation co‑benefits from better wastewater handling (less methane) and new green spaces and tree cover. Aligns with climate resilience objectives for urban areas in the Amazon region.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with Manaus’s master plan and flood‑control priority for igarapés, the Amazonas state development plan, Brazil’s housing and sanitation policies (e.g., PAC, Plansab), and IDB’s country strategy on reducing poverty and improving environmental sustainability in cities. Also supports SDGs 6 and 11.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Major risks included large‑scale resettlement, maintaining livelihoods, construction in difficult hydrological conditions, and governance/integrity challenges. Lessons: provide nearby or on‑site resettlement with free housing and strong social support; integrate housing, infrastructure, and social components; enforce robust procurement and anti‑corruption controls; pair physical works with solid waste management and environmental education to sustain creek restoration.

Project Title: Metro Line 4 Expansion (Taboão da Serra)
Sector (Categorical): Transport
Instrument: IBRD loan; PPP with private concessionaire
Funders: World Bank; State of São Paulo; ViaQuatro (PPP)
Funder (categorical): National / Subnational Government; Private / PPP; World Bank
Summary: Extension of Line 4 beyond city limits, adding stations and improving access for low‑income commuters; CO₂ reductions via mode shift.
Key Points: PPP structure; climate‑resilient design; accessibility focus.
Detailed Expected Impact: Expands São Paulo Metro Line 4 westward to Taboão da Serra, a densely populated municipality underserved by rapid transit. Improves travel time, reliability, and access to jobs for 300,000+ people. Supports mode shift from cars to rail, reducing emissions and congestion in the southwest metro area. Helps integrate transport networks and reduces inequalities in mobility.
Repayment Structure: US$400M IBRD loan to the State of São Paulo under sovereign guarantee. Financing supports public works and complements PPP funding from private concessionaires responsible for rolling stock and operations under the Line 4 PPP contract.
Operation & Maintenance: Metro Line 4 is operated and maintained by a private concessionaire (ViaQuatro) under a long-term PPP contract, with responsibility for service delivery, vehicle operation, maintenance, and fare integration.
Co-benefits (detailed): Improved access to jobs and education; increased gender and income inclusion via better mobility; local economic development around new stations; road safety from reduced car traffic; GHG reduction from mode shift to electric rail.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Residents of Taboão da Serra and western São Paulo, particularly low- and middle-income commuters who rely on public transport. Broader urban region benefits from improved network integration and reduced congestion.
Climate Goals (detailed): Supports mitigation through modal shift from high-emission road transport to low-carbon electric rail. Reduces vehicle-km traveled and emissions. Aligns with São Paulo and Brazil's low-carbon urban transport strategies.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Part of São Paulo’s urban mobility master plan and Brazil’s federal policies promoting sustainable mass transit. Aligned with World Bank urban transport and climate goals.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Land acquisition and interface with existing networks posed risks; project leveraged earlier PPP lessons from Line 4. Close coordination between public and private actors and phased construction helped manage delivery risks.

Project Title: VLT Carioca – Light Rail (Downtown)
Type of Action: Electric light rail; Bus line rationalization
Sector (Categorical): Transport
Instrument: BNDES Climate Fund; KfW finance; PPP
Amount (USD/EUR/BRL): EUR 265M (KfW); plus BNDES Climate Fund component
Funder (categorical): BNDES; KfW / BMZ; National / Subnational Government; Private / PPP
Summary: Zero‑emission LRT network integrating central intermodal nodes; reduces CO₂ and traffic.
Key Points: Climate‑friendly urban transport; strong intermodality; PPP delivery.
Detailed Expected Impact: Built 28 km of modern electric light rail (VLT Carioca) in Rio’s historic downtown, replacing polluting bus lines and creating a pedestrian-friendly corridor. Improved mobility, reduced emissions, and enhanced tourism and urban revitalization in the port area.
Repayment Structure: Financed via PPP supported by BNDES Climate Fund and EUR 265M KfW concessional loan to the municipality/state. The private concessionaire invested and is repaid via availability payments over 25 years.
Operation & Maintenance: Operated by VLT Carioca under PPP contract. Responsible for all system O&M, ticketing, and integration with buses/metro. Contract includes performance incentives and penalties.
Co-benefits (detailed): Improved air quality; noise reduction; historic preservation; increased walkability and urban regeneration in port zone. Boost to tourism and real estate markets.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Commuters, tourists, and residents in Rio’s central and port areas. Businesses and informal workers benefit from increased foot traffic and improved public realm.
Climate Goals (detailed): Mitigation through clean electric public transport; reduces GHGs and short-lived pollutants. Part of Rio’s climate plan to green its mobility system.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with Rio’s Porto Maravilha redevelopment strategy and Brazil’s climate urban transport targets, supported by federal and multilateral climate finance.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Construction delays, integration with traffic and urban renewal works posed early risks. Close coordination with port revitalization helped. PPP model ensured continuity and accountability.

Project Title: Bandeirantes Landfill Gas-to-Energy (CDM)
Sector (Categorical): Energy; Solid Waste & Circular Economy
Intermediaries/Implementers: Biogás Energia Ambiental; Prefeitura de São Paulo
Summary: Gas collection and electricity generation at Bandeirantes landfill; major CO₂e reductions.
Key Points: Early city‑level carbon finance; strong MRV through UNFCCC.
Detailed Expected Impact: Captures methane from the Bandeirantes landfill and uses it to generate electricity, reducing GHG emissions and producing clean energy. One of Brazil’s first CDM landfill projects, showcasing waste-to-energy innovation.
Repayment Structure: Funded via Clean Development Mechanism (CDM) carbon finance. Revenues from certified emissions reductions (CERs) sold to buyers like the World Bank’s carbon funds. PPP model with private operator responsible for investment and operations.
Operation & Maintenance: Private operator manages landfill gas capture and energy generation under concession. Maintains wells, flares, engines, and monitoring systems.
Co-benefits (detailed): Reduces odor and fire risks; provides renewable energy; strengthens environmental controls at landfill; technology demonstration for other cities.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Local communities near landfill benefit from reduced pollution and hazards. São Paulo gains from emissions reduction and clean energy revenue.
Climate Goals (detailed): Methane mitigation aligns with Brazil’s NDC and IPCC targets for potent GHGs. Clean energy generation adds to mitigation benefits.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Supports national climate goals and local waste management strategy. Pioneered city participation in carbon markets.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Monitoring and validation of emissions posed technical challenges. Showed value of early CDM participation and public-private innovation.

Project Title: São João Landfill Gas-to-Energy (CDM)
Sector (Categorical): Energy; Solid Waste & Circular Economy
Intermediaries/Implementers: Biogás Energia Ambiental; Prefeitura de São Paulo
Summary: Sister project to Bandeirantes capturing methane and generating electricity.
Key Points: Demonstrates scalability of LFG‑to‑energy in large metros.
Detailed Expected Impact: Captures and flares landfill methane at São João site, avoiding high-GWP emissions. One of Brazil’s key CDM urban waste projects. Demonstrates scalable GHG abatement in waste sector.
Repayment Structure: CDM-based revenue from carbon credits under performance-based contracting. Operator financed equipment and recoups cost via CER sales.
Operation & Maintenance: Private concessionaire maintains gas capture systems and monitoring under strict CDM verification protocols.
Co-benefits (detailed): Reduces explosion and odor risks; supports landfill closure and environmental compliance; builds technical capacity for methane control.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Residents near landfill; city gains GHG reduction credentials and potential carbon revenue.
Climate Goals (detailed): Direct GHG mitigation through landfill methane destruction. Supports Brazil’s low-carbon urban strategy and CDM objectives.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with city/state solid waste policies and Brazil’s climate mitigation priorities. Leveraged international carbon finance.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: CDM process complexity and monitoring costs posed barriers. Key lesson: upfront investment and expertise crucial for waste sector climate action.

Project Title: Sustainable Recovery & Flood Resilience DPL
Type of Action: Policy-based operation; Flood resilience; Early warning
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Intermediaries/Implementers: State of Rio Grande do Sul; Porto Alegre
Summary: Strengthens governance and finances resilient infrastructure and recovery post-2024 floods.
Key Points: Supports emergency fund; targeted social support; municipal resilience upgrades.
Detailed Expected Impact: Supports disaster and climate resilience reforms in Rio Grande do Sul following 2023 flooding. Strengthens early warning, flood risk governance, resilient infrastructure, and climate-adaptive planning.
Repayment Structure: US$359.6M IBRD Development Policy Loan (DPL) to the State of Rio Grande do Sul, with sovereign guarantee. Repayment over 20+ years, linked to achievement of policy actions under programmatic framework.
Operation & Maintenance: State government integrates reforms into agency mandates—e.g., environment, planning, infrastructure departments. Policies cover long-term resilience mainstreaming and system upgrades.
Co-benefits (detailed): Stronger governance for disaster preparedness; reduced social vulnerability; more transparent budgeting and asset management; gender-sensitive risk management.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Flood-prone communities, particularly low-income households in urban and peri-urban zones. Local governments receive tools and support for resilience planning.
Climate Goals (detailed): Focused on climate adaptation and disaster risk reduction. Aligns with Brazil’s NDC and Sendai Framework resilience priorities. Reduces future loss and damage from extreme weather.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Integrated into Brazil’s post-disaster response and state adaptation planning. Supports World Bank goals for climate resilience and inclusive recovery.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Reform fatigue, institutional capacity, and political transitions pose risks. Importance of sequencing actions and stakeholder engagement highlighted by earlier state reforms.

Project Title: Urban Infrastructure Insurance Facility – Recife
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management
Summary: Design of insurance solutions to protect critical infrastructure and vulnerable populations from extreme weather.
Key Points: Innovative financial instrument for cities; complements adaptation investments.
Detailed Expected Impact: The Recife Urban Infrastructure Insurance Facility (UIIF) project aims to strengthen financial resilience of the city to climate shocks. It helps Recife design, pilot, and deploy parametric insurance instruments for urban infrastructure at risk from flooding and sea-level rise. Supports long-term risk transfer strategies for critical assets, integrating climate risk data into planning.
Repayment Structure: Non-repayable grant and technical assistance funded by KfW and BMZ, coordinated by ICLEI Resilient Cities Network. Recife does not take on debt; program supports capacity building and policy design.
Operation & Maintenance: City of Recife, via its Municipal Secretariat for Climate and Sustainability and finance/planning departments, oversees risk analysis and insurance design. Long-term risk governance will be institutionalized within city structures.
Co-benefits (detailed): Financial protection for essential services and infrastructure; improved budgeting for climate contingencies; capacity building for city staff on climate risk and insurance tools.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Urban populations indirectly benefit through protection of critical services (e.g., transport, water, housing). City government gains technical and institutional capacity for climate finance and insurance.
Climate Goals (detailed): Strengthens adaptation and resilience to climate-induced disasters. Aligns with Brazil’s urban adaptation plans and ICLEI resilience goals.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Supports Recife’s Municipal Climate Action Plan and Brazil’s adaptation components under the NDC. Integrated into national disaster risk finance efforts.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Challenges include limited data and modeling for risk pricing, and institutional unfamiliarity with insurance mechanisms. Project emphasizes early-stage capacity and policy design before financial implementation.

Project Title: Linha Verde BRT & Ecological Corridor (AFD)
Sector (Categorical): Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Transport; Urban Development & Housing
Summary: New BRT line with adjacent parks and retention ponds along Barigui River to reduce flood risk.
Key Points: Integrates mobility and NBS; equitable access for underserved areas.
Detailed Expected Impact: The Linha Verde project in Curitiba combines a new BRT corridor with green infrastructure. It improves north-south urban mobility while restoring natural drainage systems and creating ecological parks. Aims to reduce floods and connect underserved neighborhoods.
Repayment Structure: AFD provided a €36.15M sovereign loan to Curitiba. City repays the loan through budgetary allocations over the term. Additional funding from the city and state government.
Operation & Maintenance: City’s public transport agency (URBS) and environment secretariat oversee BRT and park maintenance. Public-private bus operators run BRT services. Parks maintained by municipal environment and public works departments.
Co-benefits (detailed): Improved access to transport for low-income communities; flood reduction; green space creation; urban biodiversity corridors; improved air quality and health.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low-income populations in north and south Curitiba; bus commuters; residents near flood-prone areas and green park zones.
Climate Goals (detailed): Mitigation via modal shift to BRT; adaptation via flood control and green infrastructure. Supports Curitiba’s climate action plan and Brazil’s low-carbon urban strategies.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Integrated into Curitiba’s Master Plan, the National Policy on Urban Mobility, and Brazil’s NDC goals for mitigation and adaptation.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Land acquisition and coordination between transport and environment sectors required careful planning. Integrated design proved effective in combining mobility and resilience goals.

Project Title: Re‑Ciclo – E‑tricycle Door‑to‑Door Recycling
Sector (Categorical): Solid Waste & Circular Economy; Transport; Urban Development & Housing
Summary: Electric tricycles and formalization of catadores to expand recycling and improve livelihoods.
Key Points: Up to 500% income gains; 541% bikeway growth; strong replication narrative.
Detailed Expected Impact: Re-Ciclo is an e-tricycle waste collection program in Fortaleza, improving inclusive recycling while reducing GHGs from waste and transport. Trained informal waste pickers collect recyclables via electric tricycles, boosting efficiency and inclusion.
Repayment Structure: City-funded pilot program, with awards and philanthropy support (e.g., Prize for Cities). No loan repayment. Operated via city and civil society partnerships.
Operation & Maintenance: Program jointly managed by Fortaleza's Urban Environment Secretariat (SEUMA) and local NGOs. Waste pickers trained and supported in operation and maintenance of e-tricycles.
Co-benefits (detailed): Job creation; social inclusion of informal workers; GHG reduction from cleaner transport and higher recycling; improved urban cleanliness and dignity of labor.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Informal waste pickers and low-income communities; environment gains from reduced landfill and emissions.
Climate Goals (detailed): Mitigation through reduced emissions from fossil-fuel transport and increased recycling. Supports Fortaleza’s climate action plan.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with Brazil’s solid waste policy and Fortaleza’s inclusive green jobs strategy. Featured in international innovation platforms (e.g., WRI Ross Prize).
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Logistical and training barriers initially limited scale. Community co-design and iterative learning helped build trust and operational success.

Project Title: Urban Ocean – Inclusive Recycling & Circularity
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Solid Waste & Circular Economy; Urban Development & Housing
Funders: Resilient Cities Network; The Circulate Initiative; Ocean Conservancy
Summary: Integrates waste pickers into city recycling; improves sorting and livelihoods; reduces plastic leakage.
Key Points: Resilience via circularity; social equity; baseline assessments and pilots.
Detailed Expected Impact: The Urban Ocean project in Salvador supports circular economy and inclusive recycling. It improves waste separation and community waste services, particularly in coastal and vulnerable areas, to reduce marine pollution and build urban resilience.
Repayment Structure: Funded via grant and TA from Resilient Cities Network’s Urban Ocean initiative (supported by Ocean Conservancy, C40 Cities). No repayment required.
Operation & Maintenance: City agencies and local partners co-manage waste and recycling systems; focus on integrating informal sector and improving coastal community resilience.
Co-benefits (detailed): Plastic and marine pollution reduction; community health improvement; job creation in informal sector; resilience to coastal hazards; gender equity and youth engagement.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low-income coastal communities; informal waste workers; residents at risk from pollution and flooding.
Climate Goals (detailed): Mitigation through waste reduction and avoided landfill emissions. Adaptation via improved coastal waste resilience and sanitation infrastructure.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Supports Salvador’s local resilience plan and Brazil’s circular economy and coastal adaptation strategies.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Scaling inclusive recycling requires community trust and systemic investment. International peer learning and co-design proved critical.

Project Title: Parque Orla Piratininga (Jardins Filtrantes)
Sector (Categorical): Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Urban Development & Housing; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Instrument type (categorical): Grant / Technical Assistance; Municipal / Subnational Budget
Summary: 35,000 m² of filter gardens to treat runoff entering Piratininga Lagoon; creation of a large urban park.
Key Points: Biodiversity gains; water quality improvements; replicable NBS design.
Detailed Expected Impact: Restores Piratininga Lagoon using nature-based solutions (constructed wetlands). Improves water quality, biodiversity, and climate resilience while creating public green space and educational infrastructure.
Repayment Structure: Funded by municipal budget, with support from national awards, TA from ICLEI, and green infrastructure grants. No debt component.
Operation & Maintenance: City of Niterói’s Environment Secretariat oversees O&M. Wetlands and public parks maintained by municipal green infrastructure team.
Co-benefits (detailed): Flood mitigation; improved water quality; habitat creation; education and ecotourism; health benefits from cleaner lagoon and public space.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Residents of surrounding neighborhoods; city-wide visitors; schools and youth groups using the space for learning.
Climate Goals (detailed): Adaptation through ecosystem restoration; GHG reduction from carbon sequestration and avoided pollution. Recognized as a climate-nature flagship.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Supports Niterói’s green infrastructure plan and Brazil’s urban ecosystem restoration strategies. Acknowledged by AIPH Green City program.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Maintaining ecosystem balance in urban wetlands requires monitoring and adaptive management. High visibility increased public awareness and political support.

Project Title: Teresina Enhancing Municipal Governance & Quality of Life (incl. drainage, mobility)
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Transport; Urban Development & Housing; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Summary: Investments in drainage, transport corridors and city management to improve resilience and services.
Key Points: Strong evaluation docs (PAD/ICR); replicable multi‑sector design.
Detailed Expected Impact: Improves climate resilience, urban mobility, and flood management in Teresina through integrated drainage, road, and transport infrastructure. Supports institutional modernization to strengthen public service delivery and reduce vulnerability to climate-related flooding.
Repayment Structure: US$31.5M IBRD loan to the Municipality of Teresina with sovereign guarantee. Repaid through municipal budget over long term, with technical support from national programs.
Operation & Maintenance: City government departments manage transport and drainage systems post-construction. Institutional strengthening supported by digital tools and capacity building.
Co-benefits (detailed): Improved urban mobility; reduced health risks from flooding; transparency and efficiency in public financial management; inclusive urban design.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low-income neighborhoods in flood-prone areas; daily commuters; municipal departments benefiting from administrative improvements.
Climate Goals (detailed): Strengthens resilience to climate-induced floods; mitigates urban transport emissions through planning upgrades.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with Brazil’s National Policy on Climate Change and Teresina’s integrated urban development strategy. Recognized in World Bank climate finance portfolio.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Early coordination between urban planning and drainage sectors was key. Emphasized participatory planning and inter-agency cooperation.

Project Title: Metro Line 2 – Green Expansion
Sector (Categorical): Transport
Funders: World Bank; State of São Paulo
Intermediaries/Implementers: Companhia do Metropolitano de São Paulo
Summary: Extends Line 2‑Green and integrates with social housing; enhances low‑carbon access.
Key Points: Low‑carbon mobility; inclusion; integrated planning.
Detailed Expected Impact: Expands São Paulo Metro Line 2–Green by 8.3 km to the east zone, serving underserved communities. Improves access, reduces GHG emissions from road transport, and strengthens inclusive public transport infrastructure.
Repayment Structure: US$250M IBRD loan to the State of São Paulo, complemented by state and private funding under PPP model. Repayment over 20+ years with sovereign backing.
Operation & Maintenance: ViaMobilidade, a private concessionaire, operates and maintains the metro line under PPP terms. Integrates with the wider São Paulo metro system.
Co-benefits (detailed): Travel time savings; improved job access; lower air pollution; increased public safety and gender inclusion through safer stations and vehicles.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Low-income populations in São Paulo’s east zone, especially in Guaianases and Penha districts. Broader metro region benefits from connectivity.
Climate Goals (detailed): Supports modal shift to low-emission transport. Estimated GHG reduction of 38,000 tCO2e/year. Aligned with national mitigation targets.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Part of Brazil’s urban mobility investment pipeline and São Paulo’s low-carbon strategy. Featured in World Bank’s climate-tagged transport loans.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Complex land acquisition and urban interface challenges. Lessons from prior metro expansions informed mitigation measures and design sequencing.

Project Title: Climate Fund – Caieiras Landfill Biomethane/Power
Sector (Categorical): Energy; Solid Waste & Circular Economy
Summary: Climate Fund supports landfill gas capture and biomethane/power generation at Caieiras site.
Key Points: Domestic climate finance; strong mitigation; scalable model.
Detailed Expected Impact: Supports generation of biomethane and electricity from landfill gas at Caieiras landfill. Reduces methane emissions and produces renewable energy for local consumption and grid injection. Demonstrates circular economy model for waste-to-energy.
Repayment Structure: Funded by BRL 93.8M loan from BNDES Climate Fund to private concessionaire. Repayment through sale of energy and biomethane, with support from carbon credit revenues.
Operation & Maintenance: Private operator manages landfill gas capture, treatment, and conversion infrastructure. Ongoing performance monitored by environmental regulators.
Co-benefits (detailed): Reduces landfill odor and fire risk; creates green jobs; supports energy access and local air quality improvement.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Residents of São Paulo metro area; utilities and fuel distributors sourcing renewable gas; waste sector workforce.
Climate Goals (detailed): Targets short-lived climate pollutants; major GHG reduction from methane destruction. Supports Brazil’s NDC commitments on energy and waste.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligns with Brazil’s National Solid Waste Plan and renewable energy goals. Supported by BNDES climate innovation portfolio.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Technology and gas quality risks required upfront validation. Market linkage for biomethane is key to long-term viability.

Project Title: CITinova – Filtering Garden & Parque Capibaribe pilots
Type of Action: Nature‑based solutions; Water quality; Public space
Sector (Categorical): Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Urban Development & Housing; Water & Sanitation / Water Management
Summary: Large filtering garden and riverfront interventions to treat runoff and rejuvenate public space.
Key Points: Documented pilots; strong replication value; community benefits.
Detailed Expected Impact: Implements green infrastructure pilots in Recife including filtering gardens and urban park regeneration (Parque Capibaribe). Demonstrates NbS for urban water management, biodiversity restoration, and community wellbeing.
Repayment Structure: Funded by GEF via UNEP grant under CITinova program. No loan repayment; support covers TA and pilot implementation by local agencies.
Operation & Maintenance: City’s environment and planning departments manage maintenance. Community partnerships support stewardship and monitoring of NbS sites.
Co-benefits (detailed): Flood control; increased urban vegetation and recreation space; improved water quality; social cohesion and public awareness of nature-based planning.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Flood-vulnerable communities in Recife; students and local visitors; biodiversity in urban areas; low-income users of restored parks.
Climate Goals (detailed): Adaptation through urban ecosystem restoration; mitigation via urban greening and water-energy savings.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Part of Recife’s Climate Plan and national urban NbS framework supported by MMA and UNEP.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Local adaptation of international NbS concepts required. Interdisciplinary planning and local champions crucial for success.

Project Title: CITinova II – Low‑Emission Street & Urban Bioeconomy
Sector (Categorical): Energy; Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Transport; Urban Development & Housing
Summary: Pilots an integrated low‑emission street and metropolitan conservation/bioeconomy actions.
Key Points: Multi‑level governance; links city and ecosystems; replication tests.
Detailed Expected Impact: Demonstrates low-emission street design and urban bioeconomy hub in Belém’s metro region. Combines clean mobility, green infrastructure, and circular economy initiatives in pilot district, linking urban sustainability with Amazonian priorities.
Repayment Structure: Grant-funded under GEF CITinova II via UNEP/MCTI. No repayment; municipal partners co-finance implementation and maintenance.
Operation & Maintenance: City and state partners manage infrastructure and services. Local bioeconomy actors engaged in enterprise support and circular waste management.
Co-benefits (detailed): Sustainable transport; job creation in bioeconomy; urban biodiversity and flood reduction; local innovation and cultural recognition.
Primary Beneficiaries (detailed): Residents of Belém’s pilot district; informal workers and youth engaged in circular economy; environmental education beneficiaries.
Climate Goals (detailed): Mitigation via green transport and waste-to-value chain; adaptation through NbS and resilient design. Supports Amazon biome climate agenda.
Alignment with National/Local Priorities: Aligned with Pará’s green economy strategy, Belém’s climate commitments, and Brazil’s Amazon development and climate resilience goals.
Implementation Risks & Lessons: Requires deep local coordination and continuity beyond pilot phase. Emphasizes inclusive design and scaling through Amazon cities network.

Key Points: One of the first Latin American projects to link climate mitigation and urban transport; fed lessons into broader Transantiago reform.

Project Title: E-Mobility Program for Sustainable Cities (Chile component)
Type of Action: Electric public transport deployment; e-mobility planning
Sector (Categorical): Transport; Energy; Urban Development & Housing
Instrument: Blended finance program (GCF + IDB loans and grants)
Funders: Green Climate Fund; IDB; national governments
Funder (categorical): Green Climate Fund; IDB; National / Subnational Government
Intermediaries/Implementers: IDB; Chilean Ministries of Energy and Transport; local transit agencies
Summary: Provides concessional finance and TA to scale electric mobility—e-buses and supporting infrastructure—in Chilean cities as part of a 9-country regional program.
Key Points: Blends climate finance and MDB loans; addresses upfront cost barrier; couples vehicles with policy and planning support.

City: Central Chile (La Ligua / Valparaíso–Santiago supply area)
Project Title: Aguas Pacífico – Project Aqua Desalination Plant
Type of Action: Desalination for drought resilience; renewable-powered water supply
Sector (Categorical): Water & Sanitation / Water Management; Energy; Resilience & Disaster Risk Management
Instrument: Private equity investment with AIIB participation
Funders: Patria Infrastructure Fund V; AIIB; other private investors
Summary: Builds a large seawater desalination plant and pipeline to ensure resilient water supply for central Chile, powered by renewable electricity to limit GHG emissions.
Key Points: Adaptation to mega-drought with low-carbon technology; blended MDB–private finance; model for other water-stressed regions.

Project Title: Cerro Chepe Urban Reforestation (+Bosques)
Type of Action: Urban native forest restoration; erosion control; cooling
Sector (Categorical): Environment & Nature-based Solutions; Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Urban Development & Housing
Instrument type (categorical): Grant / Technical Assistance; Municipal / Subnational Budget
Funders: CONAF (+Bosques program); Municipality of Concepción; FAO (support)
Intermediaries/Implementers: CONAF Biobío; Municipality of Concepción; local communities
Summary: Plants over 6,800 native trees on 3.8 ha of Cerro Chepe to create an urban green lung, stabilize soils, and provide shade and recreation for residents.
Key Points: Shows how national climate–forestry funds can support city-level NbS; strong community buy-in for restoring a symbolic hill.

Project Title: FERI – Inclusive Recycling Enterprise Fund (Biobío)
Type of Action: Inclusive recycling; cooperative strengthening; equipment and training
Sector: Solid waste; Circular economy; Social inclusion
Sector (Categorical): Solid Waste & Circular Economy; Social Inclusion
Instrument: Corporate social responsibility fund and NGO partnership
Funders: Natura Recycling Fund; CEMPRE Chile; Fundación El Árbol
Intermediaries/Implementers: CEMPRE Chile; Fundación El Árbol; recycler cooperatives; municipalities
Summary: Provides equipment, training, and branding support to recycler cooperatives in Biobío to boost recycling rates and improve livelihoods of base recyclers.
Key Points: Ties climate mitigation in waste to social justice; private CSR catalyzes local circular economy capacity.

Project Title: Recoleta–Independencia District Energy Pilot
Type of Action: District heating & cooling; clean energy for public buildings
Sector: Building energy; Public health; Air quality
Sector (Categorical): Energy; Urban Development & Housing; Climate Mitigation
Instrument: Pre-investment studies and pilot investment via energy cooperation
Amount (USD/EUR/BRL): Part of ~US$1.17B projected national district energy portfolio
Funders: Chilean Ministry of Energy; GEF; bilateral partners (e.g., Net Zero World / US)
Funder (categorical): National / Subnational Government; GEF; Bilateral Donor
Intermediaries/Implementers: Programa Energía Distrital (Ministry of Energy); municipalities of Recoleta & Independencia; technical consultants
Summary: Pilot district energy system to provide efficient, low-emission heating and cooling to clusters of public and commercial buildings, replacing polluting individual boilers.
Key Points: Demonstrates a system-level approach to decarbonizing buildings; significant co-benefits for air quality and health.

Project Title: PULAC – Local Urban Planning for Climate Adaptation
Type of Action: Climate action planning; capacity building; risk assessment
Sector (Categorical): Resilience & Disaster Risk Management; Urban Development & Housing; Governance
Funders: UK FCDO (UK Aid) via Adaptation Research Alliance
Intermediaries/Implementers: ICLEI; UNDRR (MCR2030); Centro UC de Cambio Global; participating municipalities
Summary: Supports three Santiago municipalities to develop local climate action plans aligned with Chile’s Climate Change Law, using standardized risk tools and peer learning.
Key Points: Highlights the role of local capacity and governance in turning national climate laws into city-level action; fosters peer exchange between municipalities.
